subsystem: TensorFlow op kernel: the Compute() method of SparseDenseBinaryOpShared, the shared C++ implementation behind the SparseDenseCwiseAdd/Mul/Div family of ops. It takes a sparse tensor (as its COO triple sp_indices / sp_values / sp_shape) and a dense tensor, broadcasts the dense operand onto the sparse operand's shape, gathers the dense values that line up with each stored nonzero, and applies an elementwise binary functor (add/mul/div) between the sparse values and the gathered dense values, emitting one output value per stored nonzero.
provenance: Identified from the TensorFlow kernel idioms: the OpKernelContext *ctx parameter with a Compute(...) override, OP_REQUIRES / OP_REQUIRES_OK macros, ctx->input(...)/allocate_output/allocate_temp, TensorShapeUtils::IsMatrix/IsVector, the BCast broadcasting helper, TensorRef / Eigen::Tensor<...RowMajor>, dense_t->shaped<T,NDIM>(...).broadcast(...), FastBoundsCheck, internal::SubtleMustCopy, and DataTypeToEnum<T>. The input names "sp_indices", "sp_values", "sp_shape", "dense" and the literal string "SparseDenseBinaryOpShared broadcasts dense to sparse only" (line 45) name the op directly. The banner corpus/train/train_0304_cwe369.cpp and the header comment /* sample: primevul_validation_0033_vuln */ mark this as a PrimeVul CWE-369 (divide-by-zero) sample.
trust_boundary: All four tensors are graph inputs and are fully attacker-controllable in any setting where an untrusted party can supply a SavedModel/GraphDef or feed tensors (serving, tf.data, hosted inference). Hostile fields: the raw int64 contents of sp_shape (used verbatim to build a TensorShape at line 30), the int64 contents of sp_indices (used as gather indices at line 77), the row/column counts of sp_indices (dim_size(0) and dim_size(1)), and above all the numeric element values of the dense tensor, which become the right-hand operand of the elementwise functor at lines 106-107. The kernel is compute-only; the trust boundary is the op input interface itself.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the kernel entry point. Compute is invoked once per op execution with the runtime context ctx. It declares four const Tensor* handles and binds them to the named inputs: sp_indices (the [nnz, rank] int64 index matrix of the sparse operand), sp_values (the length-nnz vector of stored values), sp_shape (the int64 vector giving the dense logical shape of the sparse operand), and dense (the dense operand to broadcast). OP_REQUIRES_OK aborts the op cleanly if any input is missing. At this point none of the tensor CONTENTS have been validated; only presence is guaranteed.

First content check: sp_indices must be rank-2 (a matrix). This guarantees indices_t->matrix<int64>() at line 28 is well-formed and that dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) are meaningful. Crucially it constrains the tensor to 2 dimensions but does NOT constrain the SIZE of either dimension, so the row count (nnz) and the column count (index depth) remain arbitrary and attacker-chosen.

what_the_code_does.code:
OP_REQUIRES(ctx,
                TensorShapeUtils::IsVector(values_t->shape()) &&
                    TensorShapeUtils::IsVector(shape_t->shape()),
                errors::InvalidArgument(
                    "Inputs sp_values and sp_shape should be vectors "
                    "but received shapes: ",
                    values_t->shape().DebugString(), " and ",
                    shape_t->shape().DebugString()));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Second check: both sp_values and sp_shape must be rank-1 (vectors). This makes values_t->vec/flat and shape_t->vec<int64>() well-formed. Again only the rank is checked, not the element values or lengths; sp_shape may still contain any int64 values including 0 or negative numbers, and its length (the claimed rank of the sparse operand) is unbounded here.

what_the_code_does.code:
OP_REQUIRES(
        ctx, values_t->dim_size(0) == indices_t->dim_size(0),
        errors::InvalidArgument(
            "The first dimension of values and indices should match. (",
            values_t->dim_size(0), " vs. ", indices_t->dim_size(0), ")"));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Consistency check tying the two parallel arrays together: the number of stored values must equal the number of index rows (both are nnz). This is the only cross-tensor shape check. Note what is NOT checked: the column count of sp_indices (dim_size(1)) is never required to equal the rank implied by sp_shape, and the sp_shape contents are never required to be consistent with the dense tensor beyond the later broadcast-compatibility test.

Views are taken onto the raw data: indices_mat is a 2-D int64 view of sp_indices, and shape_vec is a 1-D int64 view of sp_shape. Line 30 constructs a TensorShape directly from the attacker-supplied int64 vector shape_vec and derives the sparse operand's dimension list lhs_dims from it; line 31 derives the dense operand's dimension list rhs_dims from the real dense tensor shape. Line 32 builds a BCast broadcasting plan between the two, with fewer_dims_optimization=false so the two dim lists keep the same rank. BCast computes the reshape and broadcast multipliers used later at lines 71-72.

VecGreaterEq is a local predicate that returns true only when the sparse shape lhs is at least as large as the dense shape rhs in every trailing dimension (right-to-left), i.e. the dense side broadcasts INTO the sparse side and never the reverse. The OP_REQUIRES at line 43 enforces this AND b.IsValid() (BCast succeeded / shapes are broadcast-compatible). This gate ensures the op only expands the dense operand up to the sparse shape, which is what makes the direct index reuse at lines 75-86 sound. It validates shape magnitudes but says nothing about the numeric VALUES stored in dense, nor about the column count of the index matrix.

nnz is captured as int64 from the index row count. The op allocates its length-nnz output_values tensor (output 0) and a length-nnz temporary dense_gathered of element type T. dense_gathered will hold, for each stored nonzero position, the corresponding broadcast dense value pulled in during the gather loop. Both allocations are sized purely by nnz, which is fine (non-negative), but note nnz is int64 while the gather loop counter at line 75 is a plain int.

dense_gathered_flat is a flat length-nnz view over the temporary. ndims is the rank of the sparse operand, taken from lhs_dims.size() (which came from the attacker-controlled sp_shape length). The switch on ndims dispatches to a per-rank specialization so that Eigen can operate with a compile-time NDIM. The comment asserts the reshape/broadcast index arrays have rank ndims; the gather loop relies on this to index the reshaped dense TensorRef with an NDIM-element index.

what_the_code_does.code:
#define CASE(NDIM)                                                             \
  case NDIM: {                                                                 \
    TensorRef<Eigen::Tensor<const T, NDIM, Eigen::RowMajor>> rhs_ref =         \
        dense_t->shaped<T, NDIM>(b.y_reshape())                                \
            .broadcast(BCast::ToIndexArray<NDIM>(b.y_bcast()));                \
    Eigen::array<Eigen::DenseIndex, NDIM> idx;                                 \
    bool indices_valid = true;                                                 \
    for (int i = 0; i < nnz; ++i) {                                            \
      for (int d = 0; d < NDIM; ++d) {                                         \
        idx[d] = internal::SubtleMustCopy(indices_mat(i, d));                  \
        if (!FastBoundsCheck(idx[d], rhs_ref.dimension(d))) {                  \
          indices_valid = false;                                               \
        }                                                                      \
      }                                                                        \
      OP_REQUIRES(                                                             \
          ctx, indices_valid,                                                  \
          errors::InvalidArgument("Provided indices are out-of-bounds w.r.t. " \
                                  "dense side with broadcasted shape"));       \
      dense_gathered_flat(i) = rhs_ref.coeff(idx);                             \
    }                                                                          \
    break;                                                                     \
  }
what_the_code_does.explanation: The core gather, generated once per supported rank. rhs_ref is a TensorRef that logically reshapes the dense tensor to b.y_reshape() and broadcasts it by b.y_bcast() up to the sparse shape, without materializing the expanded buffer. For each stored nonzero i and each dimension d, it reads the sparse coordinate indices_mat(i, d) (SubtleMustCopy forces a single well-defined read of the volatile-ish input), then FastBoundsCheck verifies 0 <= idx[d] < rhs_ref.dimension(d); any failure sets indices_valid=false and the OP_REQUIRES aborts before the coeff read. When all coordinates are valid, rhs_ref.coeff(idx) fetches the broadcast dense value and stores it into dense_gathered_flat(i). This is the properly-bounded part of the kernel: the dense read at line 86 is guarded. Note, however, that the inner loop reads indices_mat(i, d) for d up to NDIM-1 with no check that the index matrix actually has NDIM columns.

The macro is instantiated for ranks 1 through 5, and any other ndims falls into default which unconditionally fails the op with an InvalidArgument. This bounds the supported rank and, importantly, rejects ndims outside [1,5] BEFORE any gather runs, so the fixed-size Eigen::array<...,NDIM> idx never has a runtime-variable size. Ranks 0 and >5 (including a rank derived from an over-long sp_shape) are rejected here.

The final elementwise step: it applies the op's binary functor (Functor::func(), the add/mul/div functor this template is instantiated with) pairwise between the sparse values (values_t->flat<T>()) and the gathered dense values (dense_gathered_flat), writing nnz results into output_values on the target device. For the SparseDenseCwiseDiv instantiation the functor computes values[i] / dense_gathered[i]. Because dense_gathered holds attacker-controlled dense element values with no non-zero guarantee, and T can be an integer type, this line is where a divide-by-zero executes.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Divide-by-zero (CWE-369), the labelled defect. When this shared kernel is instantiated as SparseDenseCwiseDiv with an integer element type T (e.g. int32/int64), Functor::func() performs integer division values[i] / dense_gathered[i]. dense_gathered[i] is a value copied verbatim from the attacker-controlled dense tensor at line 86; nothing in the kernel requires those values to be non-zero. An attacker who supplies a dense tensor containing a 0 that aligns with any stored sparse nonzero triggers integer division by zero, which on the CPU is a SIGFPE / undefined behavior and crashes the process (denial of service); for float types it silently produces inf/nan but for integer types it is fatal. The invariant the implementation must uphold: before the elementwise Div it must reject (or guard) any zero denominator element of the gathered dense operand, e.g. OP_REQUIRES that no dense_gathered entry is 0 for integer Div. The add/mul instantiations are safe; this finding is latent to the Div specialization but the contract of the shared code is dangerous.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
for (int i = 0; i < nnz; ++i) {                                            \
      for (int d = 0; d < NDIM; ++d) {                                         \
        idx[d] = internal::SubtleMustCopy(indices_mat(i, d));                  \
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds read of the index matrix (CWE-125). The inner loop reads indices_mat(i, d) for d in [0, NDIM), where NDIM == ndims == lhs_dims.size() is derived from the attacker-controlled sp_shape vector. But NOTHING checks that sp_indices actually has ndims columns: the only shape checks are IsMatrix (line 10) and rows==nnz (line 23). If an attacker supplies an sp_indices matrix whose column count dim_size(1) is smaller than ndims (e.g. a [nnz, 1] index matrix together with an sp_shape of length 3, giving ndims=3), the access indices_mat(i, d) for d >= dim_size(1) reads past the row's stored columns / off the end of the backing buffer, leaking adjacent memory into idx[d]. The bogus idx value is then bounds-checked against the dense side by FastBoundsCheck, so it usually just aborts, but the read itself is already out of bounds. Invariant: the implementation must require indices_t->dim_size(1) == ndims (the sparse index depth must equal the declared rank) before the gather loop.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const auto shape_vec = shape_t->vec<int64>();
    const auto lhs_dims = BCast::FromShape(TensorShape(shape_vec));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Unvalidated shape construction from attacker data (CWE-20 / abort-based DoS, CWE-617). TensorShape(shape_vec) builds a shape directly from the raw int64 contents of sp_shape without first validating them. If any element is negative or the product of dimensions overflows the representable tensor-size limit, TensorShape's internal consistency check CHECK-fails, which calls abort() and terminates the entire process rather than returning a clean InvalidArgument. This is remotely reachable by anyone who can set sp_shape. Invariant: the implementation should validate every sp_shape element is >= 0 and that the shape is representable using the checked TensorShapeUtils::MakeShape(...) path (returning a Status) before constructing the TensorShape, so malformed shapes yield an error, not a crash.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
const int64 nnz = indices_t->dim_size(0);
    for (int i = 0; i < nnz; ++i) {                                            \
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Signed truncation of the nonzero count (CWE-190/CWE-197). nnz is an int64 taken from the index row count, but the gather loop counter at line 75 is a plain int (32-bit). For an sp_indices matrix with more than INT_MAX rows, the comparison i < nnz mixes a 32-bit i against a 64-bit nnz and the int counter overflows to a negative value, producing signed-overflow undefined behavior and either an infinite loop or writes to dense_gathered_flat(i) with a wrapped index. This requires a very large (multi-GB) index tensor so it is lower severity, but the loop bound and counter types are mismatched. Invariant: iterate with an int64 (or size-typed) counter that matches nnz.

summary: A TensorFlow SparseDenseBinaryOpShared::Compute kernel that broadcasts a dense tensor onto a sparse operand and applies an elementwise functor. The dense-side gather is properly bounds-checked (FastBoundsCheck at line 78), but three input contracts are unenforced: the elementwise functor divides by attacker-controlled dense values with no non-zero guard (integer divide-by-zero / SIGFPE crash for the Div instantiation, the labelled CWE-369 defect); the index matrix is read for ndims columns without checking it actually has that many (out-of-bounds read); and sp_shape contents are fed straight into a TensorShape that CHECK-fails/aborts on malformed dimensions. All are remotely reachable through op inputs; overall risk is denial-of-service / OOB read on untrusted models.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Standard kernel entry signature; the risk lives in what it does with the inputs, captured by the specific findings below.
Defines the four attacker-controllable inputs; their unvalidated contents drive the divide-by-zero, OOB-read and abort findings.
Legitimate rank guard on sp_indices; correct as far as it goes but only checks rank, not column count (the gap is the OOB-read finding, anchored at line 77).
Correct rank guards making vec/flat views well-formed; carry no residual contract themselves.
Correct nnz-consistency check between values and index rows; the missing sibling check (columns vs ndims) is the OOB-read finding.
Index view read at indices_mat(i,d) for d<NDIM with no column-count validation; out-of-bounds read.
Raw attacker int64 shape values feed TensorShape(shape_vec) without validation.
TensorShape built from unvalidated shape_vec can CHECK-fail/abort on negative/overflowing dims.
Derived from the real dense tensor's own (already-valid) shape; not attacker-forgeable beyond the dense shape itself.
Broadcast plan; its validity is explicitly gated by b.IsValid() at line 43, so an incompatible plan is rejected.
Correct predicate ensuring dense broadcasts into sparse only; a present, sound validation, not a defect.
Enforces broadcast-direction and BCast validity; a proper gate.
int64 nnz iterated by a 32-bit int counter at line 75 -> signed truncation/overflow for huge tensors.
Sized by non-negative nnz; allocation failures are handled by OP_REQUIRES_OK.
Rank dispatch; the default case (lines 96-101) rejects ndims outside [1,5] before any gather, so NDIM is always a fixed supported value.
Gather loop reads indices_mat(i,d) for d<NDIM with no column-count check (OOB read); dense read itself is guarded by FastBoundsCheck.
Correct bound on the dense gather coordinate; prevents OOB read/write on the dense side, a present mitigation.
Read is protected by the preceding FastBoundsCheck + OP_REQUIRES(indices_valid); write index i is within the nnz-sized temp.
Rank cap [1,5] correctly enforced before use.
Elementwise functor divides sparse values by attacker-controlled dense values with no non-zero guard -> integer divide-by-zero (the labelled CWE-369).

findings.title: Integer divide-by-zero in the elementwise Div functor over attacker-controlled dense values
findings.bug_class: Divide by zero (integer division by attacker-controlled denominator)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the functor supplied by the SparseDenseCwiseDiv registration and the Eigen binaryExpr evaluation
findings.invariant: Before applying an integer division functor, the kernel must guarantee no element of the gathered dense denominator (dense_gathered_flat) is zero, e.g. OP_REQUIRES that dense_gathered contains no 0 for integer T, or otherwise define the zero-denominator result.
findings.failure_mode: When this shared kernel is instantiated as SparseDenseCwiseDiv with an integer element type T, Functor::func() computes values[i] / dense_gathered[i]. dense_gathered[i] is copied verbatim from the attacker-supplied dense tensor (line 86) and is never required to be non-zero. Feeding a dense tensor with a 0 aligned to any stored sparse nonzero causes integer division by zero, a SIGFPE that crashes the process — a remotely reachable denial of service on any untrusted model or fed input.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm which Functor this template is instantiated with (the Div specialization) and that integer T divisions reach this line with no prior zero-denominator guard; verify the fixed kernel adds an OP_REQUIRES rejecting zero dense denominators.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of sp_indices: column count never checked against tensor rank
findings.code:
for (int d = 0; d < NDIM; ++d) {                                         \
        idx[d] = internal::SubtleMustCopy(indices_mat(i, d));                  \
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (index depth vs declared rank mismatch)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the CASE gather loop; the missing guard would live in the Validations block (lines 9-26)
findings.invariant: The implementation must require indices_t->dim_size(1) == ndims (sparse index depth equals the rank declared by sp_shape) before reading indices_mat(i, d) for d in [0, ndims).
findings.failure_mode: NDIM == ndims is derived from the length of the attacker-controlled sp_shape, but only IsMatrix and rows==nnz are checked; the column count dim_size(1) of sp_indices is never validated. Supplying an index matrix with fewer columns than ndims (e.g. [nnz,1] with a length-3 sp_shape) makes indices_mat(i,d) for d >= dim_size(1) read past each row's stored columns/off the buffer, leaking adjacent memory into idx before FastBoundsCheck runs.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no earlier check equating indices_t->dim_size(1) with ndims/lhs_dims.size(), and that indices_mat(i,d) has no internal bound on d.

findings.title: sp_shape contents fed into TensorShape without validation (CHECK-fail/abort DoS)
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation leading to CHECK-failure abort
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — TensorShape constructor invoked on raw sp_shape data
findings.invariant: Every element of sp_shape must be validated non-negative and the overall shape validated as representable (via a Status-returning path such as TensorShapeUtils::MakeShape) before constructing a TensorShape from it.
findings.failure_mode: TensorShape(shape_vec) is built directly from the raw int64 contents of the attacker-controlled sp_shape. A negative dimension or a dimension product exceeding the tensor-size limit triggers TensorShape's internal CHECK, which aborts the whole process instead of returning InvalidArgument — a remotely reachable denial of service.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no per-element validation of shape_vec precedes line 30 and that TensorShape(TensorShape) here uses the CHECK-based (non-Status) constructor.

findings.title: Signed truncation of nnz in the gather loop counter
findings.anchor: for (int i = 0; i < nnz; ++i)  with const int64 nnz
findings.bug_class: Integer truncation / signed overflow (int counter vs int64 bound)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the CASE gather loop
findings.invariant: The loop counter iterating up to nnz must be int64 (or a size type matching nnz) so it cannot overflow when nnz > INT_MAX.
findings.failure_mode: nnz is int64 (line 52) but the loop uses a 32-bit int i. For an sp_indices tensor with more than INT_MAX rows, i overflows to negative (signed-overflow UB), causing an infinite/incorrect loop and wrapped writes into dense_gathered_flat(i). Requires a multi-GB index tensor, hence low severity.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the loop counter type remains int in the shipped code and that no upstream cap keeps nnz below INT_MAX.

audit_checklist: For the SparseDenseCwiseDiv instantiation with integer T, confirm whether any zero-denominator guard exists before line 106; a fix must OP_REQUIRES no dense_gathered element is 0.
Verify a check that indices_t->dim_size(1) == ndims (== lhs_dims.size()) exists before the gather loop reads indices_mat(i, d) for d in [0, ndims).
Verify sp_shape elements are validated non-negative and representable (Status-returning MakeShape) before TensorShape(shape_vec) at line 30 to avoid a CHECK-fail abort.
Confirm the gather loop counter type is wide enough for nnz (int64) to avoid 32-bit overflow at line 75.
Confirm FastBoundsCheck at line 78 fully bounds every dense coordinate and that rhs_ref.coeff(idx) at line 86 cannot be reached with an unvalidated idx.
Confirm the default case (lines 96-101) is the only path for ndims outside [1,5] and that ndims=0 is likewise rejected before any Eigen::array<...,NDIM> is used.
